passages.explanation: Pharisees and scribes come from Jerusalem and ask: why do your disciples disobey the tradition of the elders? They don't wash their hands when they eat bread. Jesus fires back: why do you disobey the commandment of God because of your tradition? God said honour your father and mother — but you say that if anyone tells his father or mother, whatever help you would have received from me is a gift devoted to God, then he need not honour his father. You've nullified the word of God because of your tradition. Hypocrites! Isaiah was right about you: this people honours me with their lips, but their heart is far from me. They worship me in vain, teaching as doctrines the commandments of men. The Corban tradition let people declare their money as dedicated to God — while using it as an excuse to not support aging parents. Religious loopholes to avoid basic human decency.

Jesus calls the multitude: hear and understand. What goes into the mouth doesn't defile a person — what comes out of the mouth, that defiles. The disciples tell him the Pharisees were offended. Jesus responds: every plant my heavenly Father didn't plant will be uprooted. Leave them alone — they're blind guides of the blind. If the blind lead the blind, both will fall into a pit. Peter asks for an explanation. Jesus says: don't you understand? Whatever goes into the mouth passes into the stomach and out of the body. But what comes out of the mouth comes from the heart. Out of the heart come evil thoughts, murders, adulteries, sexual sins, thefts, false testimony, and blasphemies. These defile the person — eating with unwashed hands doesn't defile anyone. Jesus turns the entire purity system inside out. Contamination isn't external — it's internal.

Jesus withdraws to the region of Tyre and Sidon. A Canaanite woman from that territory cries out: have mercy on me, Lord, Son of David! My daughter is severely tormented by a demon. Jesus doesn't answer her. His disciples urge him: send her away, she keeps crying after us. He says: I wasn't sent except to the lost sheep of the house of Israel. She comes and worships him: Lord, help me. He replies: it's not appropriate to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs. She responds: yes, Lord, but even the dogs eat the crumbs that fall from their masters' table. Jesus answers: woman, great is your faith! Let it be done as you desire. Her daughter is healed from that hour. This is one of the most challenging exchanges in the gospels. The woman doesn't argue with Jesus' framing — she works within it and still presses her case. Her faith isn't deterred by what looks like rejection.

Jesus departs from there and comes near the Sea of Galilee. He goes up on a mountain and sits there. Great multitudes come to him with the lame, blind, mute, maimed, and many others. They lay them at his feet and he heals them. The multitude marvels — the mute speaking, the maimed made whole, the lame walking, the blind seeing — and they glorify the God of Israel. The phrase 'God of Israel' suggests this crowd includes Gentiles. After the Canaanite woman's breakthrough, Jesus is healing among the nations.

Jesus calls his disciples: I have compassion on the multitude — they've been with me three days now and have nothing to eat. I don't want to send them away hungry in case they faint on the way. The disciples ask: where could we get enough bread in this deserted place to satisfy such a great multitude? Jesus asks how many loaves they have. Seven, and a few small fish. He commands the multitude to sit down, takes the seven loaves and fish, gives thanks, breaks them, and gives them to the disciples to distribute. Everyone eats and is satisfied. They pick up seven baskets of leftovers. Those who ate are four thousand men, besides women and children. Jesus gets into the boat and goes to the borders of Magdala. This is the second feeding miracle — and the disciples still don't connect the dots. Same compassion, same power, same provision.
